name: Coffee Education & Training Curriculum
purpose: Builds an advanced coffee education and training curriculum that takes baristas beyond basic espresso skills into the science, craft, and culture of specialty coffee. This block creates a structured learning pathway covering green coffee, roasting principles, sensory calibration, brewing theory, milk science, and origin knowledge — transforming staff from drink-makers into coffee professionals.

detailed_objectives: Design a tiered training curriculum with clear progression from foundation to advanced coffee knowledge
Establish regular cupping sessions as the cornerstone of sensory development for all team members
Teach the seed-to-cup journey so staff understand and can communicate origin, processing, and roasting
Develop practical brewing science knowledge covering extraction theory, water chemistry, and grind calibration
Create internal assessment frameworks so skills progression is recognised and rewarded
secondary_objectives: Prepare interested baristas for external certifications such as SCA modules or City & Guilds qualifications
Build a culture where continuous learning is expected and valued, reducing staff turnover
Enable staff to educate customers confidently, increasing engagement with specialty offerings
Develop internal trainers who can cascade knowledge as the team grows

why_this_matters: The gap between a competent barista and an educated coffee professional is the gap between making drinks and understanding why those drinks taste the way they do. A barista who knows extraction theory can diagnose and fix a sour espresso in seconds rather than pulling shot after shot hoping for improvement. One who understands milk protein behaviour can texture microfoam consistently across different milk types. One who has tasted washed versus natural process coffees can recommend with genuine authority rather than reading a tasting note card. This knowledge directly impacts cup quality, waste reduction (fewer discarded shots), speed of service (confident diagnosis means faster fixes), and customer experience (knowledgeable staff command respect and build trust). The Specialty Coffee Association (SCA) estimates that trained baristas produce measurably better beverages, with customers rating drinks from certified baristas significantly higher in blind taste tests. Beyond quality, education is the single most effective retention tool in hospitality. The UK hospitality sector suffers annual staff turnover exceeding 30%, but cafes that invest in structured development programmes report dramatically lower attrition. Ambitious baristas leave jobs where they stagnate; they stay where they grow. A training curriculum signals that the business takes their development seriously and offers a career path rather than just shifts. The cost of training is a fraction of the cost of constant recruitment, and the return on investment shows up in every cup served.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Audit the current knowledge level of every team member. Use a simple skills matrix covering: espresso technique (dosing, tamping, timing, yield), milk texturing (microfoam quality, temperature control, latte art basics), grinder calibration (when and how to adjust), basic coffee vocabulary (body, acidity, sweetness, bitterness), and customer communication (can they describe today's coffee confidently?). Score each person honestly — this is diagnostic, not judgemental. Week 2: Based on the audit, assign each team member to a tier: Foundation (needs core skills reinforcement), Intermediate (solid basics, ready for deeper knowledge), or Advanced (ready for sensory calibration and brewing science). Schedule a weekly 30-minute training slot — ideally before opening on a quieter day. The first session should cover the seed-to-cup journey in 30 minutes: where coffee grows, how cherries become green beans, what happens during roasting, and why freshness matters. Use a map, sample green beans if available, and make it visual. This single session transforms how staff talk about coffee to customers. Ensure every team member can describe the current house espresso and any guest coffees in their own words, not from a script.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Develop a structured 12-week curriculum document organised by tier. Foundation tier (weeks 1-4): espresso fundamentals (dose, yield, time, taste relationship), grinder theory, milk science (protein denaturation, fat content differences across dairy and plant milks), basic sensory vocabulary using the SCA flavour wheel, hygiene and workflow. Intermediate tier (weeks 5-8): extraction theory (TDS, extraction percentage, the brewing control chart), water chemistry basics (hardness, alkalinity, pH and their impact on flavour), introduction to cupping protocol (SCA standard), origin profiles (Latin America, Africa, Asia — how geography affects flavour), and manual brew methods (V60, AeroPress, batch brew principles). Advanced tier (weeks 9-12): advanced sensory calibration (triangulation tests, threshold detection), roast profiling understanding (development time ratio, first crack), green coffee grading, blend design principles, and competition preparation if desired. Each session should be 30-45 minutes, combining theory with practical tasting. Create a Training Log for each team member tracking sessions attended, skills demonstrated, and assessment results. Identify one or two team members with teaching aptitude and begin developing them as internal trainers through a train-the-trainer programme.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Coffee education should now be woven into the cafe's identity, not treated as an add-on programme. Establish a monthly team cupping session where the whole team tastes and discusses new coffees, seasonal arrivals, or comparative tastings (e.g., the same origin processed three different ways). Support and fund SCA certification modules for committed team members — the SCA pathway includes Introduction to Coffee, Barista Skills, Brewing, Sensory Skills, Green Coffee, and Roasting, each at Foundation, Intermediate, and Professional levels. Create a visible skills progression board showing each team member's current tier and certifications. Run quarterly knowledge assessments — not as tests but as calibration exercises to identify areas for focus. Invite roasters, importers, or green coffee buyers for guest sessions when possible — exposure to the broader industry inspires and contextualises daily work. Establish a training budget line in the annual P&L so investment in education is planned and protected, not discretionary. Develop a training handbook that captures all curriculum content so it survives staff turnover and can onboard new trainers.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Conduct a skills matrix audit of every team member across core coffee competencies
Assign each team member to Foundation, Intermediate, or Advanced tier
Schedule a weekly 30-minute training slot in the team rota
Deliver the first session covering the seed-to-cup journey
Ensure every team member can describe current coffees confidently in their own words
Source basic training materials: SCA flavour wheel poster, origin map, sample green beans
Create a simple Training Log template for each team member

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Develop a structured 12-week tiered curriculum document with session plans
Introduce extraction theory sessions covering TDS, brew ratios, and the brewing control chart
Establish fortnightly cupping sessions using SCA protocol
Create practical assessments for each tier covering theory and hands-on skills
Identify and begin developing internal trainers through a train-the-trainer approach
Build origin knowledge resources covering key producing regions and processing methods
Introduce milk science sessions covering protein behaviour across dairy and plant milks

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Support and fund SCA certification modules for committed team members
Establish a monthly whole-team cupping session with structured discussion
Create a visible skills progression board displaying team member tiers and certifications
Run quarterly calibration assessments across sensory and technical competencies
Invite guest speakers from roasters, importers, or the broader coffee industry
Establish a training budget line in the annual profit and loss statement
Develop a comprehensive training handbook capturing all curriculum content

deliverables: Team Skills Matrix and audit template

tools.description: Assessment matrix scoring each team member across espresso technique, milk texturing, grinder calibration, sensory vocabulary, customer communication, and manual brew methods. Used to assign training tiers and track progression.

12-week structured curriculum template with session plans for Foundation, Intermediate, and Advanced tiers. Each session includes learning objectives, theory content, practical exercises, and assessment criteria.
Per-person log tracking training sessions attended, skills demonstrated, assessment scores, certifications earned, and development goals. Reviewed monthly during one-to-one meetings.
Step-by-step guide for running a cupping session following SCA protocol: sample preparation, water temperature, grind size, ratio, breaking the crust, and scoring using standardised cupping forms.

Calibration assessment combining written questions on coffee theory with practical blind tasting and brewing challenges. Designed to identify knowledge gaps and inform the next quarter's training focus.

failure_modes: Training sessions are scheduled but cancelled during busy periods, sending the message that education is not a priority
Curriculum is too theoretical and detached from daily work, causing staff to disengage and see training as irrelevant
Only the head barista receives advanced training while other team members stagnate at foundation level, creating a knowledge bottleneck
Training knowledge is not documented, so when the trainer leaves, the programme collapses
kpis: 100% of team members complete Foundation tier within first 3 months of employment
At least 50% of team progressed to Intermediate tier within 12 months
Weekly training sessions maintained with fewer than 10% cancellation rate per quarter
Staff retention rate measurably improved compared to pre-programme baseline
constraints: Training sessions require dedicated time that must be paid and rostered, not squeezed into breaks
SCA certification modules have associated costs (typically £150-300 per module) that must be budgeted
Cupping requires green or roasted coffee samples which may need to be sourced from the roaster partner

time_load.L1: 3 hours (skills audit, tier assignment, first session preparation and delivery)
time_load.L2: 12 hours (curriculum development, 8 training sessions at 45 minutes each, assessment creation)
time_load.L3: 4 hours/month (monthly cupping, quarterly assessments, certification support, handbook updates)

review_protocol: Weekly training log review by head barista. Monthly one-to-one development conversations between manager and each team member. Quarterly skills reassessment and tier progression review. Annual curriculum update and training budget review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block builds a structured coffee education curriculum that takes staff from competent drink-makers to knowledgeable coffee professionals. It covers sensory skills, brewing science, origin knowledge, and milk science through a tiered 12-week programme with ongoing development pathways.
implementation_risks: Training must be protected time — allowing it to be bumped by operational demands will kill the programme
Over-ambitious curriculum can overwhelm staff; start simple and build complexity gradually
Ensure training is inclusive, not elitist — every team member should feel welcome regardless of starting level
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate quality inconsistency, staff requesting development, high turnover, or owner ambition to elevate coffee standards. Pairs with B198 (Cupping & Quality Benchmarking) and B206 (Barista Competition Programme).
